expectations.text: Leads with the news (the 18% miss) in the first line or paragraph, not buildup
expectations.evidence: Opens 'Q4 came in 18% below the number. I want to walk you through what I think happened...' — news first, no buildup.

expectations.text: Explicitly chooses a failure frame and explains why
expectations.evidence: Frame stated up-front: 'Epic Fail as system breakdown + sign of the times', with explicit reasoning: 'These are the two honest frames that don't require a scapegoat.'

expectations.text: Names both causes (external softening + internal pricing change) without scapegoating
expectations.evidence: Both causes named; pricing change owned at leadership level: 'this was not a sales execution problem. The team ran the play they were given.'

expectations.text: Ends with concrete asks or next steps, not just analysis
expectations.evidence: Three numbered Q1 actions (roll back specific pricing element, stand up weekly close-rate read, re-baseline Q1 forecast).
'I'm not promising Q1 makes back what Q4 lost. I am telling you we now have a clearer read...' — calm, accountable, not self-flagellating or dismissive.

expectations.text: Produces a memo structure (sections or clear paragraphs) the user can edit, not free-flowing prose
expectations.evidence: Clean sections: Where we were going in / What knocked us down / What we learned in the hole / The climb. Plus a separate 'Notes on the draft' coda.

summary: 6/6 — explicit failure-frame choice and tactic naming are the standout differentiators.
